Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## MEASURING MASSIVE MULTITASK LANGUAGE UNDERSTANDING

|**Dan Hendrycks**|**Collin Burns**|**Steven Basart**|**Andy Zou**|
|---|---|---|---|
|UC Berkeley|Columbia University|UChicago|UC Berkeley|
|**Mantas Mazeika**|**Dawn Song**||**Jacob Steinhardt**|
|UIUC|UC Berkeley||UC Berkeley|

## ABSTRACT

We propose a new test to measure a text model’s multitask accuracy. The test covers 57 tasks including elementary mathematics, US history, computer science, law, and more. To attain high accuracy on this test, models must possess extensive world knowledge and problem solving ability. We find that while most recent models have near random-chance accuracy, the very largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average. However, on every one of the 57 tasks, the best models still need substantial improvements before they can reach expert-level accuracy. Models also have lopsided performance and frequently do not know when they are wrong. Worse, they still have nearrandom accuracy on some socially important subjects such as morality and law. By comprehensively evaluating the breadth and depth of a model’s academic and professional understanding, our test can be used to analyze models across many tasks and to identify important shortcomings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Natural Language Processing (NLP) models have achieved superhuman performance on a number of recently proposed benchmarks. However, these models are still well below human level performance for language understanding as a whole, suggesting a disconnect between our benchmarks and the actual capabilities of these models. The General Language Understanding Evaluation benchmark (GLUE) (Wang et al., 2018) was introduced in 2018 to evaluate performance on a wide range of NLP tasks, and top models achieved superhuman performance within a year. To address the shortcomings of GLUE, researchers designed the SuperGLUE benchmark with more difficult tasks (Wang et al., 2019). About a year since the release of SuperGLUE, performance is again essentially human-level (Raffel et al., 2019). While these benchmarks evaluate linguistic skills more than overall language understanding, an array of commonsense benchmarks have been proposed to measure basic reasoning and everyday knowledge (Zellers et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Bisk et al., 2019). However, these recent benchmarks have similarly seen rapid progress (Khashabi et al., 2020). Overall, the near human-level performance on these benchmarks suggests that they are not capturing important facets of language understanding.

Transformer models have driven this recent progress by pretraining on massive text corpora, including all of Wikipedia, thousands of books, and numerous websites. These models consequently see extensive information about specialized topics, most of which is not assessed by existing NLP benchmarks. It consequently remains an open question just how capable current language models are at learning and applying knowledge from many domains.

To bridge the gap between the wide-ranging knowledge that models see during pretraining and the existing measures of success, we introduce a new benchmark for assessing models across a diverse set of subjects that humans learn. We design the benchmark to measure knowledge acquired during pretraining by evaluating models exclusively in zero-shot and few-shot settings. This makes the benchmark more challenging and more similar to how we evaluate humans. The benchmark covers 57 subjects across STEM, the humanities, the social sciences, and more. It ranges in difficulty from an elementary level to an advanced professional level, and it tests both world knowledge and problem solving ability. Subjects range from traditional areas, such as mathematics and history, to more

We find that meaningful progress on our benchmark has only become possible in recent months. In particular, few-shot models up to 13 billion parameters (Brown et al., 2020) achieve random chance performance of 25% accuracy, but the 175 billion parameter GPT-3 model reaches a much higher 43 _._ 9% accuracy (see Figure 1b). On the other hand, unlike human professionals GPT-3 does not excel at any single subject. Instead, we find that performance is lopsided, with GPT-3 having almost 70% accuracy for its best subject but near-random performance for several other subjects.

Our results indicate that while recent advances have been impressive, state-of-the-art models still struggle at learning and applying knowledge from pretraining. The tasks with near-random accuracy include calculation-heavy subjects such as physics and mathematics and subjects related to human values such as law and morality. This second weakness is particularly concerning because it will be important for future models to have a strong understanding of what is legal and what is ethical. Worryingly, we also find that GPT-3 does not have an accurate sense of what it does or does not know since its average confidence can be up to 24% off from its actual accuracy. We comprehensively evaluate the breadth and depth of a model’s text understanding by covering numerous topics that humans are incentivized to learn. Since our test consists in 57 tasks, it can be used to analyze aggregate properties of models across tasks and to track important shortcomings. The test and code is available at github.com/hendrycks/test.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Pretraining.** The dominant paradigm in NLP is to pretrain large models on massive text corpora including educational books and websites. In the process, these models are exposed to information about a wide range of topics. Petroni et al. (2019) found that recent models learn enough information from pretraining that they can serve as knowledge bases. However, no prior work has comprehensively measured the knowledge models have across many real-world domains.

Until recently, researchers primarily used fine-tuned models on downstream tasks (Devlin et al., 2019). However, larger pretrained models like GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) have made it possible to achieve competitive performance without fine-tuning by using few-shot learning, which removes the need for a large fine-tuning set. With the advent of strong zero-shot and few-shot learning, it is now possible to curate a diverse set of tasks for evaluation and remove the possibility of models on “spurious cues” (Geirhos et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2019b) in a dataset to achieve high performance.
**Benchmarks.** Many recent benchmarks aim to assess a model’s general world knowledge and basic reasoning ability by testing its “commonsense.” A number of commonsense benchmarks have been

proposed in the past year, but recent models are already nearing human-level performance on several of these, including HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), Physical IQA (Bisk et al., 2019), and CosmosQA (Huang et al., 2019). By design, these datasets assess abilities that almost every child has. In contrast, we include harder specialized subjects that people must study to learn.

Some researchers have suggested that the future of NLP evaluation should focus on Natural Language Generation (NLG) (Zellers et al., 2020), an idea that reaches back to the Turing Test (Turing, 1950). However, NLG is notoriously difficult to evaluate and lacks a standard metric (Sai et al., 2020). Consequently, we instead create a simple-to-evaluate test that measures classification accuracy on multiple choice questions.

While several question answering benchmarks exist, they are comparatively limited in scope. Most either cover easy topics like grade school subjects for which models can already achieve strong performance (Clark et al., 2018; Khot et al., 2019; Mihaylov et al., 2018; Clark et al., 2019), or are focused on linguistic understanding in the form of reading comprehension (Lai et al., 2017; Richardson et al., 2013). In contrast, we include a wide range of difficult subjects that go far beyond linguistic understanding.

## 3 A MULTITASK TEST

We create a massive multitask test consisting of multiple-choice questions from various branches of knowledge. The test spans subjects in the humanities, social sciences, hard sciences, and other areas that are important for some people to learn. There are 57 tasks in total, which is also the number of Atari games (Bellemare et al., 2013), all of which are listed in Appendix B. The questions in the dataset were manually collected by graduate and undergraduate students from freely available sources online. These include practice questions for tests such as the Graduate Record Examination and the United States Medical Licensing Examination. It also includes questions designed for undergraduate courses and questions designed for readers of Oxford University Press books. Some tasks cover a subject, like psychology, but at a specific level of difficulty, such as “Elementary,” “High School,” “College,” or “Professional.” For example, the “Professional Psychology” task draws on questions from freely available practice questions for the Examination for Professional Practice in Psychology, while the “High School Psychology” task has questions like those from Advanced Placement Psychology examinations.

We collected 15908 questions in total, which we split into a few-shot development set, a validation set, and a test set. The few-shot development set has 5 questions per subject, the validation set may be used for selecting hyperparameters and is made of 1540 questions, and the test set has 14079 questions. Each subject contains 100 test examples at the minimum, which is longer than most exams designed to assess people.

Human-level accuracy on this test varies. Unspecialized humans from Amazon Mechanical Turk obtain 34 _._ 5% accuracy on this test. Meanwhile, expert-level performance can be far higher. For example, real-world test-taker human accuracy at the 95th percentile is around 87% for US Medical Licensing Examinations, and these questions make up our “Professional Medicine” task. If we take the 95th percentile human test-taker accuracy for exams that build up our test, and if we make an educated guess when such information is unavailable, we then estimate that expert-level accuracy is approximately 89 _._ 8%.
Since our test aggregates different subjects and several levels of difficulty, we measure more than straightforward commonsense or narrow _linguistic_ understanding. Instead, we measure arbitrary

Future models that use this test could be single models or a mixture of experts model. To succeed at our test, future models should be well-rounded, possess extensive world knowledge, and develop expert-level problem solving ability. These properties make the test likely to be an enduring and informative goalpost.

## 3.1 HUMANITIES

The humanities is a group of disciplines that make use of qualitative analysis and analytic methods rather than scientific empirical methods. Branches of the humanities include law, philosophy, history, and so on (Appendix B). Mastering these subjects requires a variety of skills. For example, legal understanding requires knowledge of how to apply rules and standards to complex scenarios, and also provide answers with stipulations and explanations. We illustrate this in Figure 2. Legal understanding is also necessary for understanding and following rules and regulations, a necessary capability to constrain open-world machine learning models. For philosophy, our questions cover concepts like logical fallacies, formal logic, and famous philosophical arguments. It also covers moral scenarios, including questions from the ETHICS dataset (Hendrycks et al., 2020) that test a model’s understanding of normative statements through predicting widespread moral intuitions about diverse everyday scenarios. Finally, our history questions cover a wide range of time periods and geographical locations, including prehistory and other advanced subjects.

## 3.2 SOCIAL SCIENCE

Social science includes branches of knowledge that examine human behavior and society. Subject areas include economics, sociology, politics, geography, psychology, and so on. See Figure 3 for an example question. Our economics questions include microeconomics, macroeconomics, and econometrics, and cover different types of problems, including questions that require a mixture of world knowledge, qualitative reasoning, or quantitative reasoning. We also include important but more esoteric topics such as security studies in order to test the boundaries of what is experienced and learned during pretraining. Social science also includes psychology, a field that may be especially important for attaining a nuanced understanding of humans.

- 3.3 SCIENCE, TECHNOLOGY, ENGINEERING, AND MATHEMATICS (STEM)

STEM subjects include physics, computer science, mathematics, and more. Two examples are shown in Figure 4. Conceptual physics tests understanding of simple physics principles and may be thought

We also test mathematical problem solving ability at various levels of difficulty, from the elementary to the college level. College mathematics questions, like those found on the GRE mathematics subject test, often require chains of reasoning and abstract knowledge. To encode mathematics expressions, we use LaTeX or symbols such as * and ˆ for multiplication and exponentiation respectively. STEM subjects require knowledge of empirical methods, fluid intelligence, and procedural knowledge.

## 3.4 OTHER

There is a long tail of subjects that either do not neatly fit into any of the three preceding categories or for which there are not thousands of freely available questions. We put these subjects into Other. This section includes the Professional Medicine task, which has difficult questions that require humans many years of study to master. An example is depicted in Figure 5. This section also contains business topics like finance, accounting, and marketing, as well as knowledge of global facts. The latter includes statistics about poverty in different countries over time, which may be necessary for having an accurate model of the world internationally.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

**Assessment and Models.** To measure performance on our multitask test, we compute the classification accuracy across all examples and tasks. We evaluate GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and UnifiedQA (Khashabi et al., 2020). For GPT-3 we use the OpenAI API, which provides access to four model variants, “Ada,” “Babbage,” “Curie,” and “Davinci,” which we refer to as “Small” (2 _._ 7 billion parameters), “Medium” (6 _._ 7 billion), “Large” (13 billion) and “X-Large” (175 billion). UnifiedQA uses the T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) text-to-text backbone and is fine-tuned on previously proposed question answering datasets (Lai et al., 2017), where the prediction is the class with the highest token overlap with UnifiedQA’s text output. Since UnifiedQA is fine-tuned on other datasets, we evaluate it without any further tuning to assess its transfer accuracy. We also fine-tune RoBERTa-base, ALBERT-xxlarge, and GPT-2 on UnifiedQA training data and our dev+val set. We primarily focus on UnifiedQA and GPT-3 in the rest of this document, but additional discussion of RoBERTa, ALBERT, and GPT-2 is in Appendix A.

Table 1: Average weighted accuracy for each model on all four broad disciplines. All values are percentages. Some models proposed in the past few months can move several percent points beyond random chance. GPT-3 uses few-shot learning and UnifiedQA is tested under distribution shift.

**Few-Shot Prompt.** We feed GPT-3 prompts like that shown in Figure 1a. We begin each prompt with “The following are multiple choice questions (with answers) about [subject].” For zero-shot evaluation, we append the question to the prompt. For few-shot evaluation, we add up to 5 demonstration examples with answers to the prompt before appending the question. All prompts end with “Answer: ”. The model then produces probabilities for the tokens “A,” “B,” “C,” and “D,” and we treat the highest probability option as the prediction. For consistent evaluation, we create a dev set with 5 fixed few-shot examples for each subject.

## 4.2 RESULTS

**Model Size and Accuracy.** We compare the few-shot accuracy of each GPT-3 size in Table 1. We find that the three smaller GPT-3 models have near random accuracy (around 25%). In contrast, we find that the X-Large 175 billion parameter GPT-3 model performs substantially better than random, with an accuracy of 43 _._ 9%. We also find qualitatively similar results in the zero-shot setting. While the smaller models have around 25% zero-shot accuracy, Figure 10 in Appendix A shows that the largest GPT-3 model has a much higher zero-shot accuracy of about 37 _._ 7%. Brown et al. (2020) also observe that larger GPT-3 models perform better, though progress tends to be steadier. In Figure 1b we show that non-random accuracy on the multitask test emerged with recent large few-shot models compared to datasets that assess commonsense and linguistic understanding.

To test the usefulness of fine-tuning instead of few-shot learning, we also evaluate UnifiedQA models. UnifiedQA has the advantage of being fine-tuned on other question answering datasets, unlike GPT-3. We assess UnifiedQA by evaluating its transfer performance without any additional fine-tuning. The largest UnifiedQA model we test has 11 billion parameters, which is slightly smaller than GPT-3 Large. Nevertheless, we show in Table 1 that it attains 48 _._ 9%

lowest-accuracy tasks are STEM subjects that emphasize mathematics or calculations. We speculate that is in part because GPT-3 acquires declarative knowledge more readily than procedural knowledge. For example, many questions in Elementary Mathematics require applying the order of operations for arithmetic, which is described by the acronym PEMDAS (Parentheses Exponents Multiplication Division Addition Subtraction). In Figure 7, we confirm that GPT-3 is _aware_ of the acronym PEMDAS. However, it does not consistently _apply_ PEMDAS to actual problems. On the other hand, procedural understanding is not its only weak point. We find that some verbal tasks such as Moral Scenarios from Hendrycks et al. (2020) and Professional Law also have especially low accuracy.

Our test also shows that GPT-3 acquires knowledge quite unlike humans. For example, GPT-3 learns about topics in a pedagogically unusual order. GPT-3 does better on College Medicine (47 _._ 4%) and College Mathematics (35 _._ 0%) than calculation-heavy Elementary Mathematics (29 _._ 9%). GPT-3 demonstrates unusual breadth, but it does not master a single subject. Meanhwhile we suspect humans have mastery in several subjects but not as much breadth. In this way, our test shows that GPT-3 has many knowledge blindspots and has capabilities that are lopsided.

**Calibration.** We should not trust a model’s prediction unless the model is calibrated, meaning that its confidence is a good estimate of the actual probability the prediction is correct. However, large neural networks are often miscalibrated (Guo et al., 2017), especially under distribution shift (Ovadia et al., 2019). We evaluate the calibration of GPT-3 by testing how well its average confidence estimates its actual accuracy for each subject. We show the results in Figure 8, which demonstrates that GPT-3 is uncalibrated. In fact, its confidence is only weakly related to its actual accuracy in the zero-shot setting, with the difference between its accuracy and confidence reaching up to 24% for some subjects. Another calibration measure is the Root Mean Squared (RMS) calibration error (Hendrycks et al., 2019a; Kumar et al., 2019). Many tasks have miscalibrated predictions, such as Elementary Mathematics which has a zero-shot RMS calibration error of 19.4%. Models are only somewhat more calibrated in the few-shot setting, as shown in Appendix A. These results suggest that model calibration has wide room for improvement.

## 5 DISCUSSION

**Multimodal Understanding.** While text is capable of conveying an enormous number of concepts about the world, many important concepts are conveyed mainly through other modalities, such as images, audio, and physical interaction (Bisk et al., 2020). Existing large-scale NLP models, such as GPT-3, do not incorporate multimodal information, so we design our benchmark to capture a diverse array of tasks in a text-only format. However, as models gain the ability to process multimodal inputs, benchmarks should be designed to reflect this change. One such benchmark could be a “Turk Test,” consisting of Amazon Mechanical Turk Human Intelligence Tasks. These are well-defined tasks that require models to interact with flexible formats and demonstrate multimodal understanding.

**The Internet as a Training Set.** A major distinction between our benchmark and previous multitask NLP benchmarks is that we do not require large training sets. Instead, we assume that models have acquired the requisite knowledge from reading vast quantities of diverse text from the Internet. This

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

process is typically called pretraining, but it can be thought of as training in its own right, where the downstream evaluation is demonstrating whatever knowledge we would expect a human to pick up from reading the same text.

This motivates us to propose a methodological change so that models are trained more like how humans learn. While most previous machine learning benchmarks have models learn from a large question bank, humans primarily learn new subjects by reading books and listening to others talk about the topic. For specialized subjects such as Professional Law, massive legal corpora are available, such as the 164-volume legal encyclopedia _Corpus Juris Secundum_ , but there are fewer than 5,000 multistate bar exam questions available. Learning the entire law exclusively through a small number of practice tests is implausible, so future models must learn more during pretraining.

For this reason we assess pretrained models in a zero-shot, few-shot, or transfer setting and we provide a dev, val, and test set for each task. The dev set is used for few-shot prompts, the val set could be used for hyperparameter tuning, and the test set is used to compute the final accuracy. Importantly, the format of our evaluation is not identical to the format in which information is acquired during pretraining. This has the benefit of obviating concerns about spurious training set annotation artifacts (Geirhos et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2019b) and is in stark contrast to the previous paradigm of identically distributed training and test sets. This change also enables collecting a much more extensive and diverse set of tasks for evaluation. We anticipate our methodology becoming more widespread as models improve at extracting information from diverse online sources.

**Model Limitations.** We find that current large-scale Transformers have wide room for improvement. They are notably poor at modeling human (dis)approval, as evident by the low performance on the Professional Law and Moral Scenarios tasks. For future systems to be aligned with human values, high performance on these tasks is crucial (Hendrycks et al., 2020), so future research should especially aim to increase accuracy on these tasks. Models also have difficulty performing calculations, so much so that they exhibit poor performance on Elementary Mathematics and many other STEM subjects with “plug and chug” problems. Additionally, they do not match expert-level performance (90%) on any subject, so for all subjects it is subhuman. On average, models are only now starting to move beyond random-chance accuracy levels.

Addressing these shortcomings may be challenging. To illustrate this, we attempted to create a better Professional Law model by pretraining on specialized data but achieved only limited success. We collected approximately 2,000 additional Professional Law training examples. After fine-tuning a RoBERTa-base model (Liu et al., 2019) using this custom training set, our model attained 32 _._ 8% test accuracy. To test the impact of additional specialized training data, we also had RoBERTa continue pretraining on approximately 1.6 million legal case summaries using Harvard’s Law Library case law corpus case.law, but after fine-tuning it only attained 36 _._ 1% accuracy. This suggests that while additional pretraining on relevant high quality text can help, it may not be enough to substantially increase the performance of current models.

It is unclear whether simply scaling up existing language models will solve the test. Current understanding indicates that a 10 _×_ increase in model size must be accompanied by an approximate 5 _×_ increase in data (Kaplan et al., 2020). Aside from the tremendous expense in creating multi-trillion parameter language models, data may also become a bottleneck, as there is far less written about esoteric branches of knowledge than about everyday situations.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We introduced a new test that measures how well text models can learn and apply knowledge encountered during pretraining. By covering 57 subjects at varying levels of difficulty, the test assesses language understanding in greater breadth and depth than previous benchmarks. We found that it has recently become possible for models to make meaningful progress on the test, but that state-of-the-art models have lopsided performance and rarely excel at any individual task. We also showed that current models are uncalibrated and have difficulty with tasks that require calculations. Worryingly, models also perform especially poorly on socially relevant subjects including morality and law. Our expansive test can help researchers pinpoint important shortcomings of models, making it easier to gain a clearer picture of state-of-the-art capabilities.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces MMLU, a benchmark consisting of 57 tasks across STEM, the humanities, and social sciences designed to evaluate the broad world knowledge and problem-solving capabilities of large language models in few-shot and zero-shot settings.

This work is significant because it shifts evaluation from narrow linguistic tasks to broad, expert-level academic and professional knowledge. It effectively identifies a performance 'phase change' in model scaling, specifically showing that GPT-3 sized models begin to exceed random chance where smaller models fail.

Overview issues:

- Potential Data Contamination in Training Corpora: The paper acknowledges in Section 5 that models are pretrained on 'vast quantities of diverse text from the Internet,' yet it lacks a rigorous analysis of whether the 15,908 questions in the test set appeared in the training data of GPT-3 or UnifiedQA. Since many questions are sourced from 'freely available sources online' like GRE and USMLE practice tests, there is a high probability these exact strings exist in Common Crawl or Wikipedia. Without a n-gram overlap analysis or a temporal 'held-out' validation, the claim that the benchmark measures 'knowledge acquired' rather than 'memorization of test banks' is vulnerable. The authors should perform a decontamination study by searching for test question substrings within available open-source proxies of the training data.

- Insufficient Evaluation of Prompt Sensitivity: Section 4.1 describes a fixed few-shot prompt format, but recent literature suggests that few-shot performance is highly sensitive to the specific choice of examples and their ordering. The paper does not report variance across different sets of 5-shot examples or different prompt templates. Relying on a single 'dev' set of 5 fixed examples per subject may lead to an overestimation or underestimation of a model's true capability. To address this, the authors should include an ablation study showing how accuracy fluctuates when the few-shot examples are permuted or replaced with different instances from the validation set.

- Multiple-Choice Format as a Proxy for Understanding: The methodology in Section 3 relies entirely on 4-option multiple-choice questions, where the model's prediction is determined by the highest probability among tokens 'A', 'B', 'C', and 'D'. This format may allow models to succeed through superficial pattern matching or by exploiting statistical biases in the distractors rather than through 'problem solving ability' as claimed in the abstract. The paper lacks a 'blind' baseline where models are given only the options without the question text to see if the labels themselves are biased. Including a qualitative error analysis of whether models fail due to reasoning errors or simply an inability to follow the MC format would strengthen the claims.

- Lack of Statistical Significance and Confidence Intervals: Table 1 and Figure 6 present point estimates for accuracy across 57 tasks, but there are no confidence intervals or measures of statistical significance. With some tasks containing as few as 100 examples, the margin of error could be substantial, making it difficult to determine if the 'lopsided performance' mentioned in Section 4.2 is a stable trait of the model or a result of small sample variance. The authors should provide 95% confidence intervals for the aggregate scores and the individual task scores to clarify which performance gaps are statistically meaningful.

- Ambiguity in Human Baseline and Expert-Level Comparison: Section 3 estimates 'expert-level accuracy' at 89.8% based on the 95th percentile of human test-takers, but these humans take exams under very different conditions (e.g., time limits, no access to external tools, and high-stakes pressure). The paper also mentions 'unspecialized humans' from Mechanical Turk scoring 34.5%, but it is unclear if these workers were given the same few-shot context or if they were incentivized for accuracy. A more rigorous comparison requires a controlled human study where the same interface and constraints are applied to both the model and the human participants to ensure the 'expert-level' benchmark is a fair comparison.

- Missing breakdown of performance by difficulty level: The paper claims the benchmark ranges from elementary to advanced professional levels, yet Table 1 only aggregates results by broad discipline. Without reporting accuracy specifically for the 'Elementary,' 'High School,' 'College,' and 'Professional' tiers across all subjects, the claim that the benchmark measures 'depth' remains unverified. Readers cannot determine if the GPT-3 performance jump occurs primarily on simpler tasks or if it scales across difficulty levels. The authors should include a table or plot showing average accuracy for each of these four difficulty strata to demonstrate that the benchmark effectively differentiates between basic knowledge and professional expertise.

- Lack of comparison between zero-shot and few-shot results: The abstract and introduction emphasize both zero-shot and few-shot settings, but the primary results in Table 1 and Figure 6 focus almost exclusively on few-shot performance. The paper mentions a zero-shot accuracy of 37.7% for the largest model in passing, but it never provides a task-level comparison to show where few-shot prompting actually provides a lift. This is a gap because the paper claims to measure knowledge 'acquired during pretraining,' and a large gap between zero and few-shot could suggest the model is learning the task format rather than utilizing internal knowledge. The authors should provide a side-by-side comparison of zero-shot vs. 5-shot accuracy across the 57 tasks.

- Underdeveloped discussion of calculation-heavy task failures: The authors identify that models struggle with procedural knowledge and 'plug and chug' problems in STEM, but they do not provide a systematic error analysis of these failures. It is unclear if the models fail because of incorrect arithmetic, a failure to parse LaTeX, or an inability to retrieve the correct formula. Since the paper highlights this as a major shortcoming, it needs a small-scale manual audit of errors in a task like 'Elementary Mathematics.' Specifically, the authors should categorize 50-100 errors to see if the model consistently fails at a specific step, such as the PEMDAS application mentioned in Figure 7.

- Missing verification of the 'expert-level' aggregate metric: The paper defines an expert-level accuracy of 89.8% by aggregating 95th percentile scores from various disparate exams, but it does not show how this target was computed for each of the 57 tasks. Since the tasks vary significantly in source and difficulty, a single global 'expert' number is less informative than task-specific human benchmarks. The paper would be more complete if it listed the specific human 'expert' score used for at least one representative task in each of the four categories (e.g., Professional Law vs. Elementary Math). This would allow readers to calibrate whether the 'substantial improvements' needed are uniform or if models are closer to experts in certain domains.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct a decontamination analysis to check for n-gram overlaps between the test set and common pretraining corpora.

- Report confidence intervals for all accuracy scores in Table 1 and Figure 6.

- Add a prompt sensitivity analysis showing the impact of different few-shot example selections on model performance.

- Provide a detailed breakdown of the human baseline methodology, specifically how Mechanical Turk workers were instructed and vetted.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter counts for GPT-3 model variants
comments.quote: For GPT-3 we use the OpenAI API, which provides access to four model variants, "Ada," "Babbage," "Curie," and "Davinci," which we refer to as "Small" (2.7 billion parameters), "Medium" (6.7 billion), "Large" (13 billion) and "X-Large" (175 billion).
comments.explanation: The parameter counts assigned to the OpenAI API model names appear incorrect based on the cited Brown et al. (2020) paper and OpenAI's technical documentation. In that work, the 2.7B model is a specific size, but the OpenAI API 'Ada' model is the 350M parameter version, and 'Babbage' is the 1.3B version. By mapping 'Ada' to 2.7B and 'Babbage' to 6.7B, the paper misattributes the performance of smaller models to larger architectures. The parenthetical descriptions should be updated to match the actual OpenAI API specifications: Ada (350M), Babbage (1.3B), Curie (6.7B), and Davinci (175B).

comments.title: Inconsistency in Expert-Level Accuracy Calculation
comments.quote: mple, real-world test-taker human accuracy at the 95th percentile is around  $87\%$  for US Medical Licensing Examinations, and these questions make up our "Professional Medicine" task. If we take the 95th percentile human test-taker accuracy for exams that build up our test, and if we make an educated guess when such information is unavailable, we then estimate that expert-level accuracy is approximately  $89.8\%
comments.explanation: The aggregate 'expert-level' accuracy of 89.8% is logically inconsistent with the provided example of 87% for the USMLE (Professional Medicine). If the 95th percentile for one of the most rigorous subjects in the battery is only 87%, the average across all 57 tasks can only reach 89.8% if other subjects have significantly higher expert benchmarks. However, many tasks are derived from GRE or AP exams where 95th percentile scores often correspond to near-perfect raw marks, but the paper does not reconcile how an 87% 'expert' floor in medicine leads to a higher 89.8% global average. The calculation is opaque and appears to rely on 'educated guesses' that contradict the specific data point provided. The passage should provide the specific weighted average or median of the 57 individual expert benchmarks to justify the 89.8% figure.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of GPT-3 performance relative to random chance
comments.quote: We find that while most recent models have near random-chance accuracy, the very largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average. For a 4-option multiple-choice test, random chance is 25%. Adding 20 points to this baseline would imply an average accuracy of approximately 45%. According to Table 1, the GPT-3 (175B) few-shot average is 43.9% (18.9 points above chance), but the zero-shot average is only 37.7% (12.7 points above chance). The phrasing 'almost 20 percentage points' is an overstatement when considering the zero-shot performance. The sentence should specify that this improvement refers to the few-shot setting and adjust the figure to 'nearly 19 percentage points'.

comments.title: Misinterpretation of Arithmetic Error as Procedural Failure
comments.quote: While it knows about the order of operations, it sometimes does not know how to apply its knowledge.
comments.explanation: The example in Figure 7 does not logically isolate a failure of procedural knowledge (PEMDAS) from a failure of basic arithmetic. In the expression (1 + 1) x 2, the parentheses explicitly group the addition. A failure to apply PEMDAS would typically involve ignoring the parentheses and calculating 1 + 1 x 2 = 3. If the model completes (1 + 1) x 2 with '3', it has arrived at the result that would be correct if the parentheses were absent, yet the parentheses are present in the input. This suggests the model is either ignoring the tokens for parentheses entirely or failing at the basic sum 1+1=2. The error could stem from ignoring grouping symbols rather than specifically a failure to apply the PEMDAS hierarchy.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of few-shot learning as a solution to spurious cues
comments.quote: With the advent of strong zero-shot and few-shot learning, it is now possible to curate a diverse set of tasks for evaluation and remove the possibility of models on "spurious cues" (Geirhos et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2019b) in a dataset to achieve high performance.
comments.explanation: The assertion that few-shot learning 'removes the possibility' of models relying on spurious cues is factually questionable. Research into few-shot prompting has shown that models are highly sensitive to the order of examples, the choice of labels, and common tokens in the prompt, which are themselves a form of spurious cues. While few-shot learning avoids the overfitting associated with fine-tuning on a biased training set, it introduces new prompt-based biases. The claim should be moderated to reflect that few-shot learning mitigates training-set-specific overfitting but does not eliminate shortcut learning.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of human baseline comparison
comments.quote: Additionally, they do not match expert-level performance (90%) on any subject, so for all subjects it is subhuman. On average, models are only now starting to move beyond random-chance accuracy levels.
comments.explanation: The text characterizes model performance as 'subhuman' because it fails to reach the 90% expert threshold. However, the paper also notes that 'unspecialized humans' (Mechanical Turkers) score 34.5%. Since the largest GPT-3 model achieves 43.9%, it has already surpassed this human baseline. Labeling the performance as 'subhuman' across all subjects is logically inconsistent with the reported 34.5% human baseline. The discussion should distinguish between 'sub-expert' and 'sub-human' performance levels.

comments.title: Misalignment between task description and evaluation format
comments.quote: For example, legal understanding requires knowledge of how to apply rules and standards to complex scenarios, and also provide answers with stipulations and explanations. We illustrate this in Figure 2.
comments.explanation: The text claims legal understanding requires providing 'stipulations and explanations,' but the methodology in Section 3 and the examples in Figure 2 rely strictly on a 4-option multiple-choice format. A multiple-choice selection cannot provide an explanation or a stipulation; it only identifies a label. This creates a discrepancy between the stated requirement of the domain and the actual metric used to measure it. The phrase 'and also provide answers with stipulations and explanations' should be removed because the benchmark does not test the model's ability to generate these components.

comments.title: Inconsistency in claim of near-random accuracy for law
comments.quote: Worse, they still have near-random accuracy on some socially important subjects such as morality and law.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that models have near-random accuracy on 'law,' but this contradicts the earlier statement that the largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average. If random chance is 25% for a 4-choice task, a 20-point improvement results in 45%, which is significantly above random. Table 1 shows 'Professional Law' at 41.4% (16.4% above chance). Describing these subjects as 'near-random' while describing the overall 43.9% average as a '20 percentage point improvement' is an internal inconsistency in how the authors interpret the same magnitude of delta.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of SuperGLUE saturation timeline
comments.quote: To address the shortcomings of GLUE, researchers designed the SuperGLUE benchmark with more difficult tasks (Wang et al., 2019). About a year since the release of SuperGLUE, performance is again essentially human-level (Raffel et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The text claims that SuperGLUE performance reached human levels about a year after its release, citing Raffel et al. (2019). However, the cited T5 paper reports a SuperGLUE score of 89.3, which was below the human baseline of 89.8 at the time of publication. Human-level performance on the SuperGLUE leaderboard was not actually surpassed until early 2021 by models like DeBERTa. Claiming it was 'essentially human-level' in 2019 overstates the speed of progress for that specific benchmark.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of Humanities
comments.quote: The humanities is a group of disciplines that make use of qualitative analysis and analytic methods rather than scientific empirical methods. Branches of the humanities include law, philosophy, history, and so on (Appendix B).
comments.explanation: The text defines humanities by the exclusion of scientific empirical methods, yet Appendix B categorizes 'High School Geography' and 'Health' under Humanities. Geography frequently employs empirical spatial science and quantitative GIS methods, while Health is rooted in clinical and biological sciences. This creates a logical contradiction where the section's definition of the category excludes the actual methodology of several subjects it contains. The definition should be revised to focus on human culture and social structures rather than a lack of empirical methods.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces MMLU, a benchmark designed to evaluate broad multitask language understanding across 57 diverse subjects, filling a critical gap between narrow NLP tasks and real-world knowledge. Its focus on few-shot evaluation of pretrained models aligns with contemporary usage patterns and provides a more realistic assessment of generalization. The identification of lopsided performance, poor calibration, and weak performance on socially important domains like law and morality highlights meaningful limitations in current models, offering valuable direction for future research.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for few-shot evaluation protocol: The paper assumes that few-shot evaluation is the gold standard for measuring pretrained knowledge, but does not rigorously justify why this format is superior to fine-tuning or other transfer settings for the stated goal of measuring 'knowledge acquired during pretraining.' While few-shot learning avoids dataset-specific overfitting, it conflates knowledge retrieval with prompt sensitivity. The literature context notes that MMLU scores vary up to 10% due to prompt design, yet the paper does not report multiple prompt variants or assess robustness. This undermines the reliability of the reported accuracies as stable measures of model capability. The authors should either demonstrate consistency across prompt formulations or explicitly acknowledge that performance is highly protocol-dependent.

- Task selection lacks representativeness and difficulty stratification: The paper claims broad coverage across academic disciplines but provides no principled criteria for task inclusion or difficulty calibration. The 57 tasks are listed without justification for why these domains are prioritized or how they reflect the 'real-world knowledge' humans are incentivized to learn. For example, 'Professional Law' and 'College Medicine' are included, but other high-stakes domains like engineering or public policy are absent. Moreover, difficulty is only coarsely categorized (e.g., 'Elementary,' 'College'), with no evidence that questions within a subject are equated for complexity. This risks conflating model performance with task design artifacts. A more transparent methodology would include a task selection rationale and empirical validation of difficulty levels via human baselines or item response theory.

- Accuracy metric is inadequate for diagnostic evaluation: The exclusive reliance on classification accuracy obscures important dimensions of model behavior, especially for nuanced domains like law and ethics. The paper notes poor performance on 'Moral Scenarios' but does not analyze error types or whether models fail on reasoning, values, or factual grounding. Accuracy alone cannot distinguish between a model that confidently asserts harmful actions and one that abstains—both score equally. Given that the stated concern is alignment with human values, the evaluation should include metrics like safety, consistency, or confidence calibration per task. Without richer analysis, the benchmark fails to support the very conclusions it emphasizes about ethical shortcomings.

- Human performance baseline is misleading: The paper cites 34.5% accuracy from Mechanical Turk workers as a human baseline, but this is not a valid comparison for a test designed to measure expert-level understanding. The same section estimates expert human performance at ~90%, yet the comparison is not structured to show how models lag behind domain specialists. Instead, the low Turk accuracy creates a false impression that models are close to human level when they are in fact far from expert proficiency. This undermines the paper's central claim about 'substantial improvements needed.' The authors should either compare models to domain experts or clearly separate lay versus expert human performance in the analysis.

- Lack of error analysis for low-performing domains: The paper identifies poor performance on calculation-heavy STEM subjects and socially important domains like law and morality, but offers only speculative explanations (e.g., 'procedural vs. declarative knowledge'). It does not conduct a systematic error analysis to determine whether failures stem from misreading, incorrect reasoning, or factual hallucination. For example, in the 'Professional Law' example (Figure 2), the model selects (B), which is correct, but the paper claims models perform poorly in this area without showing aggregate results. Without concrete examples and error categorization, the diagnosis remains superficial and limits the benchmark’s utility for guiding model improvement.

- Overlooked data contamination risks: The paper assumes that evaluating in few-shot settings avoids memorization, but does not address the risk that test questions or their variants may appear in pretraining corpora, especially given the public availability of practice exams for the USMLE or GRE. The literature context notes a 6.49% error rate in MMLU due to question flaws and potential leakage, yet the paper does not report any audit for data contamination. For a benchmark intended to endure, this is a critical omission. Future models may 'solve' MMLU not by understanding but by memorizing, as seen with earlier benchmarks. The authors should either clean the dataset or include a contamination analysis to ensure long-term validity.

- No evidence that benchmark avoids data contamination: The paper claims the benchmark measures knowledge acquired during pretraining, but does not rule out that model performance is inflated by memorization of test questions or their variants from pretraining data. Given that many sources are publicly available practice exams (e.g., USMLE, GRE), there is a real risk of data leakage. For a benchmark intended to be enduring and widely adopted, this is a critical gap. The authors should either conduct a contamination analysis—by searching for test questions in a snapshot of the pretraining corpus—or release a cleaned version of the dataset with duplicates removed. Without this, the validity of the reported scores as measures of generalization is questionable.

- Missing analysis of model errors in key low-performance tasks: The paper identifies poor performance on subjects like law and morality but offers only high-level speculation about causes, such as lack of procedural knowledge or misaligned values. To support claims about model shortcomings, the paper should include a qualitative error analysis on a representative sample of questions from these domains. For example, it could categorize whether errors stem from factual inaccuracies, logical flaws, or normative misunderstandings. Computing accuracy alone is insufficient; readers need to see *how* models fail in order to trust the diagnosis. Adding this would strengthen the paper’s policy implications and guide future work on alignment.

- No comparison to fine-tuned performance on same tasks: The paper positions few-shot evaluation as a test of knowledge acquired during pretraining, but never compares it to fine-tuned models on the same questions. This omission weakens the interpretability of results: it is unclear whether poor performance reflects a lack of knowledge or an inability to transfer under minimal supervision. To justify the few-shot design, the authors should evaluate at least one model family (e.g., RoBERTa) with full fine-tuning on a subset of tasks and report the performance gap. This would clarify whether the benchmark is truly measuring pretrained knowledge or just sensitivity to evaluation protocol.

- Lack of task difficulty validation via human baselines: The paper claims the test spans varying levels of difficulty but does not validate this claim with human performance across levels. For instance, there is no evidence that 'Professional Medicine' is harder for non-experts than 'High School Psychology.' To substantiate the difficulty hierarchy, the authors should report accuracy from distinct human groups—e.g., undergraduates vs. domain experts—on a subset of tasks. Without this, the interpretation of model performance by difficulty level lacks grounding, and the claim of 'broad depth' remains unsupported.

Recommendation: major revision

The paper presents a valuable and timely contribution by expanding the scope of language model evaluation, but several methodological weaknesses undermine the reliability and interpretability of its conclusions. The benchmark has strong potential, but the current evaluation design and framing require substantial refinement to support its ambitious claims.

Revision targets:

- Justify and validate the few-shot evaluation protocol with ablation studies showing performance stability across prompt variations.

- Provide a principled rationale for task inclusion and validate difficulty levels with human expert data or item-level analysis.

- Supplement accuracy with diagnostic metrics such as error types, safety, and confidence calibration, especially for law and morality tasks.

- Revise human performance comparisons to clearly distinguish lay versus expert baselines and align model evaluation with appropriate benchmarks.

- Conduct and report a data contamination analysis to ensure the test set remains valid as models scale.

comments.title: Incorrect model size comparison for UnifiedQA and GPT-3
comments.quote: The largest UnifiedQA model we test has 11 billion parameters, which is slightly smaller than GPT-3 Large.
comments.explanation: GPT-3 Large has 7.5 billion parameters according to Brown et al. (2020), so an 11 billion parameter model is larger, not smaller. This error distorts the interpretation of the size-performance relationship and undermines the credibility of the comparison. Revise the sentence to state that UnifiedQA is larger than GPT-3 Large, not smaller.

comments.title: Arithmetic inconsistency in dataset split totals
comments.quote: We collected 15908 questions in total, which we split into a few-shot development set, a validation set, and a test set. The few-shot development set has 5 questions per subject, the validation set may be used for selecting hyperparameters and is made of 1540 questions, and the test set has 14079 questions.
comments.explanation: The reported numbers do not sum correctly: 57 subjects × 5 = 285 development examples; 285 + 1,540 + 14,079 = 15,904, but the total collected is stated as 15,908. This discrepancy of four questions raises concerns about data handling transparency. Either adjust the test set size to 14,083 or clarify how the remaining questions are accounted for. Internal consistency is essential for a benchmark intended to be enduring.

comments.title: Mathematically incorrect example in PEMDAS illustration
comments.quote:
$(1 + 1)\times 2 = 3$

![img-6.jpeg](img-6.jpeg)
Figure 7: GPT-3's completion for two prompts testing knowledge of the order of operations. The blue underlined bold text is the autocompleted response from GPT-3. While it knows about the order of operations, it sometimes does not know how to apply its knowledge.
comments.explanation: The expression $(1 + 1) \times 2$ evaluates to 4, not 3. Presenting this as GPT-3's output without verification risks misrepresenting both the model's behavior and the correct application of PEMDAS. If this is an actual model completion, include a verbatim log or screenshot to confirm authenticity. Otherwise, correct the arithmetic or clarify that it is a hypothetical illustration.

comments.title: Misleading claim about few-shot evaluation eliminating spurious cues
comments.quote: With the advent of strong zero-shot and few-shot learning, it is now possible to curate a diverse set of tasks for evaluation and remove the possibility of models on "spurious cues" (Geirhos et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2019b) in a dataset to achieve high performance.
comments.explanation: The claim that few-shot evaluation removes spurious cues misapplies the cited literature. Geirhos et al. (2020) and Hendrycks et al. (2019b) focus on annotation artifacts in fine-tuned vision and NLP models, not on prompt-based biases in few-shot learning. In fact, few-shot performance is known to be sensitive to prompt phrasing, example order, and answer formatting—introducing new forms of spurious correlation. Replace this claim with a more accurate statement acknowledging that while few-shot evaluation avoids direct memorization, it introduces other potential biases.

comments.title: Unverified model output presented as empirical evidence
comments.quote:
$(1 + 1)\times 2 = 3$

![img-6.jpeg](img-6.jpeg)
Figure 7: GPT-3's completion for two prompts testing knowledge of the order of operations. The blue underlined bold text is the autocompleted response from GPT-3.
comments.explanation: The paper presents this completion as evidence of GPT-3’s behavior but provides no verifiable log, trace, or screenshot confirming that this exact output was generated by the model. In scientific evaluation, claimed model behaviors must be reproducible. Without such verification, the example risks being anecdotal or fabricated, especially given its mathematically incorrect result. Include a genuine inference log or qualify the example as illustrative rather than empirical.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of PEMDAS precedence rules
comments.quote: the acronym PEMDAS (Parentheses Exponents Multiplication Division Addition Subtraction)
comments.explanation: This description implies a strict left-to-right hierarchy where multiplication precedes division and addition precedes subtraction, which is incorrect. In standard arithmetic, multiplication and division have equal precedence and are evaluated left to right; the same applies to addition and subtraction. For example, $8 \div 2 \times 4 = 16$, not $1$. To avoid misleading readers, revise the description to clarify that operations of equal precedence are resolved left to right.

comments.title: Conflation of acronym recall with conceptual understanding
comments.quote: In Figure 7, we confirm that GPT-3 is aware of the acronym PEMDAS. However, it does not consistently apply PEMDAS to actual problems.
comments.explanation: Reproducing the string 'PEMDAS' and its expansion does not demonstrate 'awareness' or understanding—it shows only surface-level recall. The model may have memorized the sequence without linking it to arithmetic computation. Since the paper aims to distinguish declarative from procedural knowledge, it should avoid anthropomorphic language like 'aware' and instead state that GPT-3 can reproduce the acronym but fails to apply the corresponding rules consistently.

comments.title: Missing analysis of left-to-right associativity in arithmetic
comments.quote: For example, many questions in Elementary Mathematics require applying the order of operations for arithmetic, which is described by the acronym PEMDAS (Parentheses Exponents Multiplication Division Addition Subtraction).
comments.explanation: The paper claims to assess procedural knowledge via PEMDAS but provides no examples involving operations of equal precedence (e.g., division before multiplication), where left-to-right evaluation is required. A problem like $12 \div 3 \times 2$ tests whether the model applies division first (correct: $4 \times 2 = 8$) rather than deferring to multiplication due to the acronym's order. Without such cases, the evaluation does not fully probe procedural mastery and may conflate pattern matching with reasoning.

comments.title: Improper calibration metric used
comments.quote: We evaluate the calibration of GPT-3 by testing how well its average confidence estimates its actual accuracy for each subject. We show the results in Figure 8, which demonstrates that GPT-3 is uncalibrated. In fact, its confidence is only weakly related to its actual accuracy in the zero-shot setting, with the difference between its accuracy and confidence reaching up to  $24\%$  for some subjects.
comments.explanation: The paper uses the absolute difference between confidence and accuracy as a proxy for miscalibration, but this is not a standard or reliable measure. Proper calibration assessment requires binned reliability diagrams or expected calibration error (ECE), which evaluate whether confidence scores align with accuracy across the full range of predictions. A single pointwise difference does not establish poor calibration. Replace the current analysis with ECE or reliability plots to support the claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about data bottleneck for esoteric knowledge
comments.quote: data may also become a bottleneck, as there is far less written about esoteric branches of knowledge than about everyday situations.
comments.explanation: The claim that data scarcity will bottleneck performance on esoteric topics goes beyond what the cited scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020) support. Those laws assume data quality and diversity but do not establish that low frequency of text on specialized subjects translates to insufficient learning signal. Model failures could stem from task difficulty, poor alignment, or evaluation design rather than raw data volume. Remove or qualify this claim unless supported by corpus analysis showing coverage gaps.

comments.title: Overinterpretation of legal task performance as rule-following ability
comments.quote: Legal understanding is also necessary for understanding and following rules and regulations, a necessary capability to constrain open-world machine learning models.
comments.explanation: The benchmark assesses only multiple-choice selection, not the ability to generate explanations or apply rules in novel contexts. A model could memorize correct answers without possessing general rule-following competence. The leap from test performance to real-world constraint capability is unjustified. Remove the second clause unless the paper provides evidence of transfer to open-ended rule application.

comments.title: Inconsistent model evaluation conditions in Table 1
comments.quote: We also fine-tune RoBERTa-base, ALBERT-xxlarge, and GPT-2 on UnifiedQA training data and our dev+val set.
comments.explanation: Table 1 presents RoBERTa, ALBERT, and GPT-2 alongside GPT-3 and UnifiedQA without indicating that the former were fine-tuned while the latter were evaluated in few-shot or transfer settings. This creates a misleading apples-to-oranges comparison. The caption should clearly annotate which models were fine-tuned and which were evaluated zero-shot or few-shot to ensure fair interpretation.

comments.title: Uniform 25% random baseline assumes unverified four-choice format
comments.explanation: The random baseline assumes all questions have exactly four answer options, but the paper does not confirm this across all 57 tasks. If any task has fewer or more options, the expected random accuracy would differ (e.g., 33.3% for three choices). Without verifying uniform option count, the 25% baseline is unjustified and may distort relative model gains. Either confirm the four-way format or compute a weighted baseline per task.

comments.title: Ambiguous prediction method for UnifiedQA
comments.quote: the prediction is the class with the highest token overlap with UnifiedQA's text output.
comments.explanation: The term 'token overlap' is ambiguous—it could mean Jaccard similarity, longest common subsequence, or unnormalized count. Without specifying the exact matching algorithm (e.g., exact string match, ROUGE-L), the evaluation lacks reproducibility. Clarify the metric used, as different choices can yield different predictions, especially when options have similar surface forms.

comments.title: Non-monotonic GPT-3 performance contradicts scaling expectations
comments.explanation: GPT-3 Medium (6.7B) achieves lower average accuracy (24.9%) than GPT-3 Small (2.7B, 25.9%), contradicting the expected trend of improved few-shot performance with scale. While not impossible, this anomaly should be acknowledged or investigated—especially since larger variants show increasing performance. Possible causes include inconsistent prompting or tokenization, but no explanation is provided. Address this non-monotonicity in the discussion.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces MMLU, a benchmark for evaluating language models across 57 diverse academic subjects. While the benchmark fills an important gap by testing models' knowledge beyond commonsense, several methodological concerns limit its validity as a comprehensive measure of language understanding. The most significant issues involve potential data contamination, arbitrary task selection, and insufficient statistical rigor.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by creating a broad benchmark that reveals important limitations in current language models, particularly their lopsided performance and poor calibration. The finding that even the largest models struggle with procedural knowledge and socially relevant subjects is significant. However, the benchmark's validity is compromised by methodological issues that could artificially inflate or deflate performance measurements.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Analysis of Data Contamination: The paper acknowledges that models were pretrained on massive internet corpora, yet the test questions were collected from 'freely available sources online' including practice exams. There is no rigorous analysis of whether test questions appeared in training data, which could significantly inflate performance. The authors only mention in passing that this evaluation 'obviates concerns about spurious training set annotation artifacts' without providing evidence. Given that models like GPT-3 were trained on vast portions of the internet, including educational content, this is a critical validity threat. The paper should include a systematic analysis of potential contamination, perhaps by checking for exact matches or near-duplicates between test questions and known training data sources.

- Arbitrary Task Selection Without Justification: The paper presents 57 subjects but provides no principled justification for why these specific topics were chosen or how they represent the full spectrum of human knowledge. The selection appears opportunistic based on available questions rather than systematic. The authors state the subjects span STEM, humanities, social sciences, and 'other areas' but don't explain the sampling strategy or why some important domains are missing. Without clear selection criteria, it's difficult to assess whether the benchmark comprehensively measures 'language understanding' or merely reflects the availability of multiple-choice questions online. The paper should articulate a principled framework for task selection and discuss how the chosen domains map onto important real-world applications of language understanding.

- Lack of Statistical Significance Testing: The paper reports accuracy differences across models and tasks but provides no statistical significance testing. With 57 tasks and multiple models, some observed differences could be due to chance. For example, the claim that GPT-3 X-Large 'performs substantially better than random' with 43.9% accuracy versus 25% baseline needs statistical validation. Similarly, the conclusion that models have 'lopsided performance' across subjects requires testing whether observed differences are statistically significant. The paper should include confidence intervals, p-values, or other statistical measures to support its claims about performance differences. This is particularly important given the relatively small number of test examples per task (minimum 100).

- Limited Analysis of Prompt Sensitivity: The paper uses fixed 5-shot prompts for evaluation but provides minimal analysis of how sensitive results are to prompt variations. The authors state they 'create a dev set with 5 fixed few-shot examples for each subject' but don't explore how different prompt constructions or example selections might affect performance. Given the known sensitivity of few-shot learning to prompt design, this is a significant methodological gap. The paper should include ablation studies showing how performance varies with different prompt formulations, numbers of examples, or example selection strategies. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether the measured performance reflects the models' capabilities or artifacts of the specific prompt design.

- Questionable Assumption About Uniform Task Difficulty: The paper treats all 57 subjects as comparable in its analysis, averaging accuracies across domains without accounting for varying difficulty levels. The authors note that 'Human-level accuracy on this test varies' but don't adjust for this when comparing model performance. For example, a 70% accuracy on an easier subject might not represent the same level of understanding as 40% on a harder subject. The paper should analyze relative task difficulties (perhaps using human performance as a benchmark) and consider weighted or normalized scoring to make fairer comparisons across subjects. This would strengthen claims about which subjects models truly struggle with versus which are inherently more difficult.

- Missing demonstration of benchmark's discriminative power: The paper claims its benchmark can help researchers pinpoint model shortcomings, but provides no concrete example showing how the benchmark reveals specific failures that other benchmarks miss. For instance, the paper should include a case study where a model performs well on existing benchmarks like SuperGLUE but fails dramatically on a specific MMLU subject, demonstrating the benchmark's unique diagnostic value. Without such examples, readers cannot assess whether the benchmark truly offers new insights beyond what existing evaluations already provide.

- No worked example of prompt engineering protocol: The paper describes using few-shot prompts but provides no detailed specification of how prompts were constructed or optimized. Given the known sensitivity of few-shot learning to prompt design, the paper should include a complete worked example showing the exact prompt format, template, and few-shot examples used for at least one representative subject. This would enable reproducibility and help readers understand the methodological choices that affect performance.

- Missing analysis of benchmark's practical utility: The abstract claims the benchmark helps identify 'important shortcomings of models' but the paper never demonstrates how researchers would use these insights to improve models. The paper should include a concrete example showing how performance patterns across the 57 tasks informed a specific model modification or training strategy, and whether this led to measurable improvements. Without this connection between evaluation and model development, the benchmark's practical impact remains speculative.

- Insufficient validation of expert-level performance estimates: The paper estimates expert-level accuracy at 89.8% but provides minimal justification for this figure. The authors should compute and report actual expert performance on a subset of questions, perhaps by administering the test to domain experts or using official exam statistics where available. Without empirical validation of the expert baseline, claims about how far models are from human expertise remain unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct systematic data contamination analysis to verify test questions don't appear in training corpora

- Provide principled justification for task selection and analysis of domain coverage

- Add statistical significance testing for all performance comparisons and claims

- Include prompt sensitivity analysis showing robustness of results to prompt variations

- Analyze and adjust for varying task difficulty levels across subjects

comments.title: Mathematical error in order of operations example
comments.explanation: The equation shown in Figure 7 contains a clear mathematical error. The calculation (1 + 1) × 2 equals 4, not 3. This is a basic arithmetic mistake that undermines the example's credibility. The authors should correct this to $(1 + 1) \times 2 = 4$ to maintain accuracy in their demonstration of GPT-3's procedural knowledge failures.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for SuperGLUE human-level performance
comments.quote: To address the shortcomings of GLUE, researchers designed the SuperGLUE benchmark with more difficult tasks *(Wang et al., 2019)*. About a year since the release of SuperGLUE, performance is again essentially human-level *(Raffel et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: The citation *(Raffel et al., 2019)* is incorrect for the claim that SuperGLUE performance reached human-level. Raffel et al. 2019 is the T5 paper "Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer," which does not discuss SuperGLUE performance reaching human-level. The human-level performance claim for SuperGLUE should cite either the original SuperGLUE paper *(Wang et al., 2019)* if it made this claim, or a subsequent paper that documented models achieving human-level performance on SuperGLUE. Replace *(Raffel et al., 2019)* with the appropriate citation that actually documents SuperGLUE models reaching human-level performance.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated confidence measurement methodology
comments.quote: Worryingly, we also find that GPT-3 does not have an accurate sense of what it does or does not know since its average confidence can be up to  $24\%$  off from its actual accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paper makes a specific quantitative claim about GPT-3's confidence calibration without explaining how confidence was measured. For multiple-choice questions, it's unclear whether confidence was derived from token probabilities, temperature sampling, or some other method. The claim needs to specify: (1) the exact method for extracting confidence scores from GPT-3's outputs, (2) the dataset and tasks over which this 24% deviation was computed, and (3) whether 'up to 24%' refers to a maximum observed deviation or an average across tasks. Without this methodological detail, the claim cannot be verified or reproduced. Add a paragraph after this sentence describing the confidence measurement methodology.

comments.title: Question count arithmetic inconsistency
comments.quote: We collected 15908 questions in total, which we split into a few-shot development set, a validation set, and a test set. The few-shot development set has 5 questions per subject, the validation set may be used for selecting hyperparameters and is made of 1540 questions, and the test set has 14079 questions.
comments.explanation: The arithmetic doesn't add up. With 57 subjects and 5 questions per subject for the few-shot development set, that's 285 questions. Adding the validation set (1540) and test set (14079) gives 285 + 1540 + 14079 = 15904 questions total, but the paper claims 15908 questions were collected. There's a discrepancy of 4 questions that needs explanation. Rewrite the sentence to state "We collected 15904 questions in total" or clarify where the additional 4 questions are allocated.

comments.title: Inconsistent random baseline calculation
comments.explanation: The random baseline shows 25.0% across all categories, but this assumes all questions have exactly 4 choices. The paper doesn't specify whether all questions in the 57 subjects have 4 options each. If some questions have different numbers of choices, the random baseline should vary accordingly. For example, if some questions have 3 choices, the random baseline would be 33.3%, not 25.0%. The paper should either verify that all questions have exactly 4 choices or adjust the random baseline calculation to account for varying numbers of options per question.

comments.title: Contradiction in GPT-3 evaluation methodology
comments.quote: For GPT-3 we use the OpenAI API, which provides access to four model variants, "Ada," "Babbage," "Curie," and "Davinci," which we refer to as "Small" (2.7 billion parameters), "Medium" (6.7 billion), "Large" (13 billion) and "X-Large" (175 billion). UnifiedQA uses the T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) text-to-text backbone and is fine-tuned on previously proposed question answering datasets (Lai et al., 2017), where the prediction is the class with the highest token overlap with UnifiedQA's text output. Since UnifiedQA is fine-tuned on other datasets, we evaluate it without any further tuning to assess its transfer accuracy. We also fine-tune RoBERTa-base, ALBERT-xxlarge, and GPT-2 on UnifiedQA training data and our dev+val set.
comments.explanation: The text states that UnifiedQA is evaluated 'without any further tuning to assess its transfer accuracy,' but then immediately says 'We also fine-tune RoBERTa-base, ALBERT-xxlarge, and GPT-2 on UnifiedQA training data and our dev+val set.' This creates confusion about whether UnifiedQA was fine-tuned on the MMLU data. The sentence structure suggests the fine-tuning applies only to RoBERTa, ALBERT, and GPT-2, but this should be clarified. The authors should restructure this paragraph to make clear that UnifiedQA was NOT fine-tuned on MMLU data, while the other three models were.

comments.title: Undefined weighted accuracy calculation
comments.quote: Table 1: Average weighted accuracy for each model on all four broad disciplines. All values are percentages.
comments.explanation: The table header mentions 'Average weighted accuracy' but the paper never explains how the weighting is calculated. Are the disciplines weighted by number of questions, number of subjects, or some other metric? Without this definition, readers cannot interpret whether the 'Average' column fairly represents overall performance or if some disciplines are given disproportionate influence. The authors should specify the weighting scheme used to compute these averages.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance for performance claims
comments.quote: We find that the three smaller GPT-3 models have near random accuracy (around  $25\%$ ). In contrast, we find that the X-Large 175 billion parameter GPT-3 model performs substantially better than random, with an accuracy of  $43.9\%$ .
comments.explanation: The claim of 'substantially better than random' performance requires statistical validation. With 57 tasks and varying sample sizes (minimum 100 examples per task), the 18.9 percentage point improvement from 25% to 43.9% could occur by chance. The paper should provide confidence intervals or p-values to demonstrate statistical significance. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the observed improvement is meaningful given the variance across tasks and sample sizes. Add statistical significance testing results after the accuracy comparison to support the 'substantially better' claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated expert-level performance baseline
comments.quote: It shows the both models are below expert-level performance for all tasks, with GPT-3's accuracy ranging from  $69\%$  for US Foreign Policy to  $26\%$  for College Chemistry.
comments.explanation: The paper claims models perform 'below expert-level performance for all tasks' without providing empirical evidence of actual expert performance on these specific questions. Without measuring how human experts perform on the exact test questions, the claim is unsubstantiated. The authors should either report expert performance on a subset of questions or cite official exam statistics where available. Add expert performance benchmarks for each task category to validate the 'below expert-level' comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent model size comparison claim
comments.quote:
edQA model we test has 11 billion parameters, which is slightly smaller than GPT-3 Large. Nevertheless, we show in Table 1 that it attains  $48.9\%$

![img-5.jpeg](img-5.jpeg)
Figure 6: GPT-3 (few-shot) and UnifiedQA results.

accuracy. This performs better than the few-shot GPT-3 X-Large model, despite UnifiedQA have an order of magnitude fewer parameter
comments.explanation: The text creates confusion by comparing UnifiedQA to both GPT-3 Large and X-Large in the same sentence.
